model_narrative: We run an airline that has several types of aircraft and a set of routes to serve. We decide how many aircraft of each type to assign to each route, and given those assignments we determine how many passengers are actually carried and how many end up bumped on each route under each possible demand scenario. Operating the aircraft costs money, and bumping passengers who wanted to travel costs us lost revenue. The goal is to choose the assignments and resulting passenger flows so that the total operating cost plus the total expected bumping cost is as small as possible.

sets.name: i
sets.doc: the available aircraft types, each a distinct fleet of planes that can be flown on routes

sets.name: j
sets.doc: the flight routes that the airline serves

sets.name: h
sets.doc: the discrete demand states, indexing the possible passenger demand scenarios that can occur on a route

params.name: dd
params.doc: the passenger demand on a route under each demand state, in number of passengers

params.name: lambda_
params.doc: the probability that a given demand state occurs on a route; for each route the probabilities over the demand states sum to one

params.name: c
params.doc: the cost of operating one aircraft of a given type on a given route, in thousands of dollars

params.name: p
params.doc: the passenger seating capacity that one aircraft of a given type provides on a given route, in number of passengers

params.name: aa
params.doc: the number of aircraft of each type that are available to assign

params.name: k
params.doc: the revenue lost per bumped passenger on a route, in thousands of dollars per one hundred passengers bumped

params.name: ed
params.doc: the expected passenger demand on a route, computed as the probability-weighted average of the demand across the demand states

params.name: deltb
params.doc: the incremental passenger load that becomes carryable when reaching a demand state on a route, that is the additional passengers between this demand state and the previous one

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the number of aircraft of each type assigned to each route

vars.name: y
vars.doc: the number of passengers actually carried on a route in each demand state

vars.name: b
vars.doc: the number of passengers bumped on a route in each demand state, meaning those who demanded travel but were not carried

vars.name: oc
vars.doc: the total operating cost of the aircraft assignment, in thousands of dollars

vars.name: bc
vars.doc: the total expected bumping cost from passengers who could not be carried, in thousands of dollars

constraints.name: x[i,
constraints.intent: The number of aircraft we put into service is limited by how many we own. For each aircraft type, the total number of that type assigned across all the routes cannot exceed the number of that type that we have available.

constraints.name: y[j,
constraints.intent: We can only carry as many passengers as the assigned planes have seats for. For each route and each demand scenario, the passengers actually carried on that route cannot exceed the total seating capacity provided by all the aircraft assigned to that route.

constraints.name: b[j,
constraints.intent: Anyone who wanted to travel but was not carried counts as bumped. For each route and each demand scenario, the bumped passengers equal that scenario's demand on the route minus the passengers actually carried.

constraints.name: oc
constraints.intent: The total operating cost comes from flying the assigned aircraft. Set the operating cost equal to the sum over every aircraft type and route of the per-aircraft operating cost times the number of aircraft of that type assigned to that route.

constraints.name: bc
constraints.intent: The total expected bumping cost reflects the lost revenue from bumped passengers, weighted by how likely each scenario is. Set the bumping cost equal to the sum over every route and demand scenario of the route's per-passenger bumping penalty times the probability of that scenario times the passengers bumped on that route in that scenario.

constraints.name: y[j,
constraints.intent: The passengers carried on a route should not run ahead of the demand that has actually materialized. For each route and each demand scenario, the passengers carried cannot exceed the incremental passenger load associated with that demand state on that route.

query: Ops wants to keep the operation lean — let's look at holding the total number of aircraft we deploy across all routes to no more than 25 and see what that does to the plan.
expected_pyomo: model.fleet_cap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x[i, j] for i in model.i for j in model.j) <= 25)

query: There's a service-commitment proposal on the table for route-5 — guarantee at least 100 seats flown across the demand states. What would that cost us?
expected_pyomo: model.route5_service = Constraint(expr=sum(model.y['route-5', h] for h in model.h) >= 100)

query: An airworthiness directive is pulling three of our type-a jets for inspection next cycle — at most 7 of them can be deployed in total. At the same time, sales has already committed a block on route-1 that needs at least 220 seats of capacity flown there. Both land in the same cycle — what does expected cost come to?
expected_pyomo:
model.a_grounded = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x['a', j] for j in model.j) <= 7)
model.r1_cap_floor = Constraint(expr=sum(model.p[i, 'route-1'] * model.x[i, 'route-1'] for i in model.i) >= 220)

query: Every single route is flying with fewer seats than its expected demand — a couple of them are barely covered at all. Shouldn't each route be required to fly seat capacity for at least half of that route's expected demand? What math makes systematic under-capacity the right call?

query: Across the network we're stranding over a thousand passengers in expectation every cycle. Shouldn't the plan hold the probability-weighted total of bumped passengers — summed over all routes and demand states — under 980? What's the tradeoff that keeps the bumping that high?
expected_pyomo: model.bump_budget = Constraint(expr=sum(model.lambda_[j, h] * model.b[j, h] for j in model.j for h in model.h) <= 980)

query: Route-4 is bumping a ton of passengers across its states and that can't be good for us. Couldn't we keep total route-4 bumping under 300? Walk me through what's pulling it the other way.
expected_pyomo: model.route4_bump_cap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.b['route-4', h] for h in model.h) <= 300)

params.doc: the expected passenger demand on a route, computed as the probability-weighted average of the demand across the demand states

params.name: deltb
params.doc: the incremental passenger load that becomes carryable when reaching a demand state on a route, that is the additional passengers between this demand state and the previous one

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the number of aircraft of each type assigned to each route

vars.name: y
vars.doc: the number of passengers actually carried on a route in each demand state

vars.name: b
vars.doc: the number of passengers bumped on a route in each demand state, meaning those who demanded travel but were not carried

vars.name: oc
vars.doc: the total operating cost of the aircraft assignment, in thousands of dollars

vars.name: bc
vars.doc: the total expected bumping cost from passengers who could not be carried, in thousands of dollars

constraints.name: x[i,
constraints.intent: The number of aircraft we put into service is limited by how many we own. For each aircraft type, the total number of that type assigned across all the routes cannot exceed the number of that type that we have available.

constraints.name: y[j,
constraints.intent: We can only carry as many passengers as the assigned planes have seats for. For each route and each demand scenario, the passengers actually carried on that route cannot exceed the total seating capacity provided by all the aircraft assigned to that route.

constraints.name: b[j,
constraints.intent: Anyone who wanted to travel but was not carried counts as bumped. For each route and each demand scenario, the bumped passengers equal that scenario's demand on the route minus the passengers actually carried.

constraints.name: oc
constraints.intent: The total operating cost comes from flying the assigned aircraft. Set the operating cost equal to the sum over every aircraft type and route of the per-aircraft operating cost times the number of aircraft of that type assigned to that route.

constraints.name: bc
constraints.intent: The total expected bumping cost reflects the lost revenue from bumped passengers, weighted by how likely each scenario is. Set the bumping cost equal to the sum over every route and demand scenario of the route's per-passenger bumping penalty times the probability of that scenario times the passengers bumped on that route in that scenario.

constraints.name: y[j,
constraints.intent: The passengers carried on a route should not run ahead of the demand that has actually materialized. For each route and each demand scenario, the passengers carried cannot exceed the incremental passenger load associated with that demand state on that route.

query: Route-5's expected demand is five times route-2's, yet route-2 ends up with five times the seats. Is there a reason route-5 shouldn't be flying at least as much seat capacity as route-2? What's the model seeing that I'm not?
expected_pyomo: model.r5_match_r2 = Constraint(expr=sum(model.p[i, 'route-5'] * model.x[i, 'route-5'] for i in model.i) >= sum(model.p[i, 'route-2'] * model.x[i, 'route-2'] for i in model.i))
